instructional_goal: Given the STM32N6 family datasheet and a set of face recognition access control terminal requirements, you will evaluate the STM32N6x5xx and STM32N6x7xx device variants and produce a justified hardware architecture specification that identifies the appropriate variant, maps the camera-ISP-NPU data pipeline, selects security features for biometric data protection, and defines the memory layout needed to support real-time facial recognition inference.
audience: Firmware engineers responsible for evaluating and integrating the STM32N6 microcontroller into biometric access control products, specifically those assessing device variant suitability and architecting facial recognition pipelines
conditions: Working from the STM32N6x5xx/STM32N6x7xx production datasheet (DS14791) as the primary reference
Given a defined product requirement: a face recognition access control terminal requiring real-time inference, camera input, and secure biometric data handling
Operating in a pre-implementation hardware evaluation and architectural planning context, prior to firmware coding or PCB layout
tools_and_resources: Peripheral functional overview sections (NPU, ISP/DCMIPP, SAES, CRYP, MCE, XSPI, SRAM)
Power and memory architecture diagrams (Figure 16, SRAM configuration section)

in_scope: Comparing STM32N6x5xx (crypto, no NPU) vs STM32N6x7xx (NPU, no crypto) feature sets and selecting the appropriate variant for a face recognition terminal
Evaluating the Neural-ART NPU subsystem (600 Gops, DNN inference) for real-time facial biometric workloads
Mapping the camera/ISP pipeline (CSI-2, DCMIPP, ISP pipes, DMA to NPU) for real-time face capture and preprocessing
Assessing security features (TrustZone, SAES, CRYP, HASH, MCE, PKA) and memory architecture (TCM, AXISRAM, XSPI) for biometric data protection and low-latency inference
out_of_scope: Writing or debugging firmware source code for the STM32N6
PCB schematic or layout design for the STM32N6
Detailed configuration of communication peripherals unrelated to the biometric pipeline (e.g., CAN, Ethernet, USB)
Production testing, qualification, or reliability analysis of the STM32N6 device

assumptions: Learners have working knowledge of ARM Cortex-M architecture and general MCU peripheral concepts (timers, DMA, SPI, I2C, GPIO)
Learners are familiar with embedded camera interfaces (parallel or MIPI CSI-2) and basic image signal processing concepts at a conceptual level
Learners understand the general concept of neural network inference on embedded hardware but are not yet familiar with the STM32N6 Neural-ART NPU specifically
Learners have not previously worked with the STM32N6 family and need to build familiarity with its variant differentiation and peripheral architecture from the datasheet
Learners can read and interpret hardware datasheets, including feature tables, block diagrams, and electrical characteristics sections

evidence.relevance: Describes the Neural-ART accelerator (600 Gops, DNN inference, streaming engines, weight decompression) available only on STM32N6x7xx — directly supports NPU evaluation for face recognition
Introduces the NPU (neural processing unit) core as a runtime-reconfigurable neural network inference engine, supporting the goal of evaluating NPU suitability for biometric workloads
Describes the camera/ISP pipeline including CSI interface, DCMIPP, ISP processing pipes, and direct DMA feed to the NPU — core to architecting the camera-to-inference pipeline
Details the camera subsystem dual-path architecture (DCMI and DCMIPP), CSI-2 and parallel interfaces, and pixel rates — essential for camera pipeline architecture decisions
Describes the SAES coprocessor (available only on STM32N6x5xx) with SESIP/PSA Level 3 certification — critical for the variant selection tradeoff between NPU and crypto
Describes the CRYP cryptographic processor (AES, available only on STM32N6x5xx) — supports security feature assessment for biometric data protection
Describes the Memory Cipher Engine (MCE) for inline encryption/decryption of external memory — relevant to securing biometric data stored in external flash
Describes SRAM configuration with ECC, hardware/software erase — supports memory architecture planning for the inference pipeline
Describes the Cortex-M55 core with Helium vector processing, TCM RAM sizes, and FPU — foundational for understanding compute resources available for the biometric pipeline
Feature/peripheral count table for STM32N655xx showing crypto (SAES, CRYP, PKA, MCE) present but Neural-ART absent — directly supports variant comparison
Feature/peripheral count table for STM32N647xx showing Neural-ART present but crypto absent — directly supports variant comparison for the core design tradeoff
Describes the XSPI interface supporting external serial memories (NOR flash, HyperRAM) in memory-mapped mode — relevant to external memory architecture for model weights and frame buffers

confidence_rationale: The datasheet provides comprehensive coverage of all the technical domains the persona needs to evaluate (NPU, ISP/camera, security, memory). The persona's pain points map directly to identifiable sections of the document. The 2-hour duration is realistic for a structured evaluation exercise (not implementation). The goal is performance-oriented, bounded, and tied to a concrete real-world deliverable (architecture specification with variant selection justification).
questions_for_sme: Is there a planned or existing STM32N6x7xx variant that includes both the Neural-ART NPU AND the full crypto engine (SAES, CRYP, PKA, MCE), or is the NPU/crypto tradeoff a permanent architectural constraint of the current product family?

What is the expected minimum face recognition inference latency requirement for a commercial access control terminal (e.g., <500ms end-to-end), and is 600 Gops sufficient for the target neural network model size and architecture?

Are there reference designs, application notes, or STM32Cube firmware examples specifically demonstrating the camera-ISP-NPU pipeline on the STM32N6 that learners should be directed to alongside this datasheet?

For the 2-hour course duration, should the variant selection decision be treated as a case study exercise (learners produce a written recommendation), or is the goal purely conceptual evaluation without a deliverable artifact?

What security certification requirements (e.g., FIDO, ISO/IEC 30107 for PAD, GDPR biometric data handling) are relevant to the target access control product, and how do they map to the STM32N6 security features covered in this datasheet?
